method2.generated_article:
==Etymology==
Tromelin Island, known in French as Île Tromelin, was formerly called the Isle of Sand.[1] It is named in honour of Jacques Marie Boudin de Tromelin de La Nuguy, captain of the French corvette Dauphine.[1] Tromelin reached the island on 29 November 1776 and rescued eight enslaved Malagasy people who had been stranded there for 15 years.[1]

==Description==
Tromelin Island is a low-lying, flat coral island of approximately 1 square kilometre in the Indian Ocean, about 450 kilometres east of Madagascar and 500 kilometres north of Réunion.[4] It is administered by France as part of the Scattered Islands district within the French Southern and Antarctic Lands.[4] The island is uninhabited except for a meteorological station that has operated since 1949 and supports cyclone monitoring and regional weather forecasting.[4] It has been described as a “desert island”, with a few low bushes and no trees.[5]

The island’s coral-sand landscape is influenced by aeolian sedimentation, with wind-blown sand contributing to its topography.[3] On Tromelin, wind speeds exceeding 8 metres per second occur mainly from directions between 100° and 140°.[3]

Tromelin formerly supported a variety of native bird and plant species, but its ecosystem was severely damaged after rats were accidentally introduced.[1] Six of the island’s eight native bird species could not withstand the invasive predators, and rats heavily consumed native plants.[1] Before eradication, only a few hundred pairs of masked and red-footed boobies remained among the seabirds.[2] French authorities eradicated the rodents in 2005 using poisoned bait, after which native plants began to recover and three bird species returned to the island.[1][2] The island is also used as a sea turtle sanctuary and supports efforts to protect nesting sea turtles from invasive species and habitat threats.[4][5]

==Fauna and flora==
Tromelin Island's fauna is dominated by seabirds, while nesting green sea turtles are the main non-avian vertebrates; the island has no native terrestrial mammals, reptiles or amphibians.[1] Before the introduction of invasive Norway rats, the island supported six to eight seabird species in large numbers, but by 2005 only two booby species still bred there with limited success.[1][2] Following rat eradication in 2005, the seabird community recovered naturally, increasing to seven breeding species and from 353 to 4,758 breeding pairs by 2022.[1] The recovery included the masked booby, represented on Tromelin by the endemic subspecies Sula dactylatra melanops.[1] Between 2017 and 2021, eleven non-breeding seabird species were also recorded, including frigatebirds using the island as a regular roost and tropicbirds showing behaviour that suggested possible future settlement.[3] Mice are now the only introduced mammals present on the island.[3]

The island's vegetation is sparse and low-growing, reflecting its small coralline surface, strong winds, salt spray and limited freshwater.[1] Its native flora was historically simple, consisting mainly of the littoral shrub Tournefortia argentea, also known as tree heliotrope, and fewer than ten herbaceous species, with no native trees.[1] Invasive rats heavily reduced native vegetation by eating seeds, seedlings and foliage, but native plants began to recover after eradication efforts in 2005–2006.[1][2] Monitoring after eradication recorded an increase in native flora to seven taxa by 2013, including greater density and height of Tournefortia argentea.[1] A small number of introduced coconut palms persist on the island, but they do not dominate the native plant community.[1] Ongoing conservation focuses on preventing new invasive introductions so that the recovery of seabirds and vegetation can continue.[1][4]

==History==
The island was discovered by France in the 1720s, recorded by the French navigator Jean Marie Briand de la Feuillée, and named Île de Sable, meaning “Isle of Sand”.[1]

In July 1761, the French ship Utile was wrecked near Tromelin while carrying an illicit cargo of enslaved people.[2] Twenty sailors drowned in the wreck, along with at least seventy enslaved people who were unable to escape from below deck because the hatches had been locked or nailed down.[2] A small group of survivors remained stranded on the island for fifteen years.[2]

On 29 November 1776, the ship Dauphine arrived at the island, and Captain Chevalier de Tromelin rescued seven women and a young child, who were taken to Mauritius and later freed.[1][3] The island was subsequently named Tromelin in honor of Captain de Tromelin.[3] France’s claim to sovereignty over Tromelin dates from the arrival of the Dauphine in 1776.[1]

Archaeological investigations of the wreck and the island have uncovered traces of the castaways’ survival, including walls of dwellings built from coral blocks and compacted sand, as well as a large oven containing remains of turtles, birds, and shellfish.[2] Earlier British accounts also recorded remnants of stone houses and neatly arranged graves on the island.[2] The story of the stranded survivors has since become a symbol of slavery, abandonment, survival, and human resilience, and has been documented through archaeological reports, articles, and the documentary Tromelin, the Island of Forgotten Slaves.[5]

Mauritius bases its claim to sovereignty on the argument that Tromelin should have been ceded to the United Kingdom under the Treaty of Paris of 1814 and should not have remained administered by France as a dependency of Réunion.[1] The United Nations has never recognized Mauritian sovereignty over Tromelin.[1] In 1954, France built a meteorological station and landing strip on the island, strengthening its administrative presence there.[1]

==Climate==
Tromelin Island has a tropical savanna climate (Köppen Aw), with consistently warm temperatures and a distinct wet season from November to April influenced by the intertropical convergence zone.[2] The average annual temperature is about 26.7 °C, with little seasonal variation because of the island’s low-latitude oceanic setting.[2] Annual precipitation is approximately 1,074 mm, concentrated in the summer months; February is generally the wettest month, while conditions are drier from May to October.[2] Prevailing southeast trade winds are common, often exceeding 8 m/s, and contribute to high evaporation and persistent breezy conditions.[2] The island is well placed for meteorological observation in the western Indian Ocean, particularly for monitoring cyclones affecting Madagascar, and its weather station remains in operation.[4]
